narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36, your undergraduate GPA sits at 2.50, which is below the typical 3.21 average for students in this prediction system, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both rank at 2, notably lower than the averages of around 5.7 and 5.5. Your family comes from a lower-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of students. The model assessed your overall profile and predicted a 56.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales just enough to result in the rejection of your law school application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed on that key milestone. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar, thanks to its competitive standing close to the average. However, your Year 3 decile ranking carried the most significant negative weight, strongly nudging the model toward predicting failure because it reflects grades that placed you in the lower end of your school's distribution. Your undergraduate GPA followed as the next biggest concern, also contributing toward the failure prediction since it falls short of what's typical for successful bar takers. Compared to other students, your academic deciles and GPA stand out as weaker than average, which overshadowed the strengths in your LSAT and full-time status, ultimately driving the borderline decision against you. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a modest background—many in similar situations have improved with targeted effort—but the model weighs these patterns from past data heavily. To move forward, focus on bolstering your GPA through postgraduate courses or a master's program to lift those decile rankings, retake the LSAT if possible to push it even higher, and gather strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Consider part-time options or bridge programs at less selective schools to build a proven track record. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could flip the prediction in your favor, showing growth beyond the initial metrics. You're close, and with strategic steps, law school remains within reach.
